rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE CHARLES B. WILSON DTD 4/13/88 REVOCABLE TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: Petition To Determine That The Last Two Amended And Restated Trust Instruments, And The Last Trust Amendmentm Each Of Which Modified The April 13,1998
rulings.Rulings: Petitioner's request that Respondent Frances Petrocelli be removed as trustee of the Charles B. Wilson Revocable Trust, and a temporary trustee be appointed to administer the trust during the pendency of the litigation, is GRANTED, under Probate Code §§ 15642 and 17206. Under Getty v. Getty (1988) 205 Cal.App.3d 134, a trustee may be removed due to a conflict of interest between the trustee and the beneficiaries. If there is a legally proper ground for removal, "there need be no evidence of actual past wrongdoing by the trustee to justify his removal," because, "The purpose of removing a trustee is not to inflict a penalty for past action, but to preserve the trust assets." (205 Cal.App.3d 139, 140). The Getty court held that a temporary trustee with no conflicts of interest is "in a better position to guide the trust through litigation." (205 Cal. App. 3d 142). Here, there is a clear conflict of interest. Petitioner, a beneficiary, is the son of the Grantor. Respondent, Trustee, Grantor's spouse, is the mother of the competing beneficiaries. Respondent attempts to distinguish Getty, and cites Copley v. Copley (1981) 126 Cal.App.3d 248 to argue that when the settlor knew of the potential conflict of interest at the time of appointment, a trustee can only be removed on showing of actual misconduct by the trustee. The trustee in Getty also argued under Copley that a trustee cannot be removed except for "extreme grounds" (205 Cal.App.3d 141). In response, the Getty court stated "there [was] no showing that the trustor knew of any potential conflict of interest. Cases stating the Copley rule rely on the conflict of interest being fully known to the appointing party." (Id.) So it is here; there is no showing that the conflict of interest between Respondent as trustee and Petitioner as a beneficiary was "fully known" to Mr. Wilson at the time the challenged instruments were executed. Indeed, Petitioner alleges that Respondent intentionally hid from Mr. Wilson the changes to the Trust that favored her own biological family and disfavored Mr. Wilson's children. Moreover, there were no allegations of undue influence or lack of capacity to execute the challenge trust instruments in Copley, as there are here. Further, the Copley Court stated, "Hostility, antagonism and inevitable future conflict can justify an order of removal of the trustee when these factors impair the proper administration of the trust" (126 Cal.App.3d 248, 288, quoting Estate of Gilmaker (1962) 57 Cal.2d 627, 632). Respondent, as Trustee, is vested with authority under the Trust to make unilateral decisions about distribution of Trust assets during her lifetime and use of Trust funds to defend against challenges to the Trust and to trust instruments. The conflict of interest between Respondent and Petitioner is not merely over who will control the Trust and who will benefit from the Trust's assets. Respondent's papers cast personal aspersions against Petitioner; Petitioner alleges undue influence against Respondent. The current hostility, antagonism, and conflicts of interest between Petitioner and Respondent would likely impair proper administration of the Trust and Respondent's duty as Trustee to treat all beneficiaries neutrally, and most acutely during the pendency of the litigation. With Respondent's removal as trustee during the pendency of litigation and appointment of a neutral temporary trustee, Petitioner's request that Respondent be enjoined from using Trust assets to pay for the litigation, or alternatively to be ordered to show the Court that attorney's fees charged to the Trust are reasonable, is moot. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) and opposing parties no later than 4:00 p.m. Tuesday, April 9, 2019.
